sentences.text: --almost 15 years.
Hmm.
I know I used to go--
--Ojai, Santa Barbara, Santa Maria--
I went as far as Paso Robles.
That was my territory that I was travelling for years and years, once a month, in the [? rug ?] industry.
Well, after we were marching those 21 days of death march, we arrived.
They, they put us on a train.
It was an open car.
It wasn't closed.
It was open, like a flat bed.
And we were traveling on that one for-- I don't know how long.
It seemed like forever because it was freezing, absolutely freezing cold.
And then one day, we just arrived to Buchenwald.
And I was just happy to be there, even though I found out a half hour later that this is another death camp.
And we were there until April-- towards the end of April.
How I stayed alive, I have absolutely no idea-- cruelty upon cruelty, just undescribable.
People died, left and right.
In, in-- inside, inside the barrack, where, where you-- where you slept-- I want to call it a bed.
But it isn't a bed.
I mean, it's just, just planks of wood.
And you were sleeping row on row on-- you know, right next to each other.
Every morning, two or three just didn't get up.
They were dead.
And, and one time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I was almost grabbed to take a hold of his feet or, or head.
And for some reason, I just quickly mushed into the half-darkness.
Because it was always still dark inside.
Otherwise, I would have had to hold that person and carry him out with another person.
Always two people carried a dead person out.
And I, I saw dead people next to me left and right, all the time.
And that is in, in, in my nightmares many times.
Faceless people-- because I didn't know any of them in person.
But how they died, how nobody cared is just unbelievable.
And--
Oh, yeah.
We heard that.
Three days before we actually got liberated, the Swiss Red Cross was allowed to stop.
And every single prisoner got a small little package.
That's not.
That was-- it may have been already in May, yeah.
Because I got liberated May the 8th.
And we got-- we got a little package of, like, a K-ration.
And we were told, do not eat all of it at one time.
Just ration yourself, or else you get dysentery.
And sure enough, some people didn't listen.
And they didn't make it.
And then we were in-- inside the train that was traveling for eight days and eight nights, and so on.
And then one morning, everything is at a standstill.
The train is standing there for hours.
And it's just started to get daylight.
And somebody looks out the window and says, hey, there's a strange uniform out there.
Must be a Polish uniform.
And he says-- another one, no, that looks like a Russian uniform.
I said, pick me up!
Pick me up!
Because the window was very high, you know.
And so somebody picked me up.
He says, this is an American soldier.
It's an Eisenhower-- and we saw one Eisenhower jacket with a Tommy gun-- one soldier, nobody else.
And minutes later, all the doors opened up-- and, like, 2,000 prisoners out of those wagons.
And the Germans started to tear off their ranks and threw down their guns-- and holding their hands high in the air.
And imagine, 2,000 people-- at least 2,000 people running towards that one American soldier.
I would have kissed his ass, too.
I just couldn't get close enough.
And within minutes, it was swarming with Americans, you know.
And then they were holding us back from beating up on the German.
Because some of them picked up the, the guns.
And with the butt, they started hitting the Germans that just tore off their ranks, you know.
And they were-- they were saving their lives.
Anyway, about a couple hours later, a major makes an announcement in English, in Yiddish, in a couple other languages, that you are free now.
You are welcome to stay here as long as you want to beef up your, your appearance, in other words, to get yourself back into shape.
The kitchen will be open 24 hours.
You can eat all the time.
But don't eat more than every two hours.
You can eat a little bit every two hours.
If you eat too much, you're going to get sick.
And you can die.
And some of them did.
Because wouldn't listen.
They couldn't stop themselves.
They died of dysentery.
This was at a-- at a-- I think we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a camp.
[SIGHS] It must have been a DP camp.
But I-- it wasn't the same kind of barracks that we were sleeping in.
It must've been the German camp, or wherever-- wherever the German soldiers were, were living.
This is about 3 kilometers outside of Munich.
And I was building myself up.
I ate every two hours.
I mean at 2 o'clock in the morning, at 4 o'clock in the morning, at 6:00 in the morning-- every two hours, just a little bit, remembering what the major told me.
And then one day, I went over to him.
And I said, I would like to make my way back to my hometown.
He says, we have trucks leaving every day to Pilsen.
Just get on one of those trucks.
I still have my suit on, the prison suit.
I got into Pilsen.
And there, we ran into a cousin that was just-- he got liberated in, in January already.
That's Andy's cousin, the one that-- that's coming here.
He gave us a bunch of money, gave us a bunch of cigarettes.
And he told us, don't go back to Munkacs.
Nobody's going back there because the Russians are occupying it.
You come to Sighet, which is the place where they took me from.
Two of the Kaufmans-- two of the Kaufmans are, are there.
They made it.
Of the three boys-- the three sons-- remember, I told you my aunt was asking my father to send me one of your boys because my three sons were drafted.
The two boys made it back.
The third one that was with me in concentration camp, he never made it back.
So anyway, that-- that's where we went back.
But from Pilsen, I had to get on a train to go to Prague.
I was still alone, not knowing anyone.
Oh, wait a minute.
That was in Prague where we met my cousin that give us money and all that.
I got on a train in Pilsen going to Prague, which was a few hours' travel.
I was allowed to travel, free of charge.
I laid down in one of those-- it's like-- in, in Europe they have trains, first class, second class, and so on.
I didn't care what class, but it must have been better than third class.
I just laid down across a bench.
And I fell asleep.
I woke up.
And I see six people-- there was room for five people to sit.
Six people opposite me are squishing together.
And they didn't want to wake me up-- Czechoslovakians.
No sooner did I wake-- did I wake, I quickly sat up, you know.
And one of them hands me a piece of bread yea big-- home-baked bread with marmalade on it.
And he says, you must be hungry, you know.
And, and, and this all went in Czech.
I had the striped suit on.
Yes.
And I had no other clothes.
And not only that, I, I, I had my arm tattooed.
They see it.
And they see how thin I was.
I think I weighed 78 pounds the last day of the war, 78 pounds.
So these, these were Czech people.
They were-- they were nice.
And this, this is another interesting story.
I was walking on the streets of Prague and, and stopping at a-- at a restaurant, just looking through the window, looking at the-- at the foods, and whatnot.
And there stands in, in the entrance, by the doorway, stands-- must've been the chef because he had one of those white hats on.
And he says to me, are you hungry?
I, I said, yes.
Come on in.
Well, I came in.
He gave me a full-course dinner.
And he says, come back tomorrow.
I wound up eating with this guy for I don't know how many days like that.
And then he invited me to his house.
And when I got to New York, I sent him a package of-- like, a care package.
His wife wanted nylon stockings.
That was a big deal over there.
And I, I kept sending him, like, three or four times packages like that.
And then one time his wife wrote me a letter that he died.
His-- you know, the guy that dragged me in from the street.
This was just a, a little human-interest story.
The Czech people, generally, were nice people, even the ones that didn't like Jews.
They were nice people.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes.
Yes, especially in-- when living in Czechoslovakia.
Anyway from Romania, I was making my way into-- one day, my cousin, who was, like-- he wasn't chief of police.
But the-- if chief of police was out of town, he was chief of police.
He lives in Montreal now.
And we lived in-- that's Kaufman.
We lived in his house.
And one day, he said, I think you guys have start-- better start making your way back to Czechoslovakia because the Russians are starting to look at papers.
They're starting to look at passports, and so on, you know.
Because it was free going and coming.
And so this cousin of mine, who lives in North Hollywood, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the two of us, made-- we decided we're going to make our way to come to America.
And that's how we came back to Prague.
But in order to get to Prague, you have to have papers.
And you have a passport.
You have to cross the Hungarian border.
We did.
And they were shooting after us at night.
We hired a guide to take us through, unofficially.
You know, in other words, we didn't-- we didn't cross the border officially with papers, and so on.
But I had money sewn into my jacket pocket-- American dollars, Czechoslovakian money, Hungarian money, and so on.
So we sort of made it that way.
And when they were asking for papers on the train, we made believe we're sleeping.
And that, that way, they left you alone.
And one way or another, we made our way to Prague, where we had a cousin that was married, and she lived there.
In fact this was Andy's sister-- the Andy that lives here, Andy Meisels.
And we lived-- and then one day, we got a telegram from my uncle, that there's $500 waiting for you at the Czechoslovakian National Bank.
And somebody advised us, do not take the $500 out all at once because they give it to you in Czech money.
And from day to day, it changes.
So we sort of, like, lived-- for $10, another $10.
We lived off of that for six months, even going to nightclubs a few times, eating in restaurants.
And after six months, which was 1947 in May-- March-- we got to New York, I still had $40 out that $500.
And I want to give it back to my uncle.
[LAUGHTER] He says-- [LAUGHS] he started laughing.
He says, keep it for pocket money.
[LAUGHS] He was really nice, you know.
He-- when he saw the, the nightmares, the crying that I did, and all that, he says, why are you sitting always in the house?
I came home from school.
And I sat in the house.
He had a television set.
People came-- neighbors came to watch television.
That was in 1947.
It was-- Milton Berle has just started.
And he used to give me, like, $20.
Why don't you go out and have some fun?
Here's the keys to my car.
Go take out a girl, you know.
Go have some fun.
He had a brand new Cadillac.
He gives me his keys.
I didn't know how to drive-- [LAUGHTER] --which he didn't know.
I quickly went to learn how to drive.
Yeah, yeah.
He was-- he was nice.
If they have, I did not notice.
One German guard-- this was during the death march-- one German guard-- one day, he goes, psst!
And I didn't like to be singled out for anything.
He goes at me, singles me out-- psst!
Finally, I says-- I go close to him, yes?
Here, hide it.
He gives me a piece of bread.
I thought, my god, what happened to this guy, you know?
He was a volunteer Hungarian that became a German SS.
And he was a guard.
He heard me talk Hungarian to this friend of mine that wanted to sit down.
And I wouldn't let him.
And that's why he singled me out.
In other-- I guess-- I just surmise that that's how [SIGHS] he singled me out.
[LAUGHS] So that was the only softness that I saw that in a German.
I hated all of them.
[CRYING]
[CRYING] That I love them.
[CRYING] And I miss them.
[CRYING] I'm not going to answer that question.
I'm going to start crying.
And I don't want to.
[CRYING]
Ah, I met my wife in 1962-- September 8th.
And we got engaged September 18, 10 days later.
And we got married December 8.
That's another-- a funny story in itself.
I'm in my office all by myself.
In walks a woman.
And she says, I have a big request to make of you.
I am the director of a-- the parochial school, a Jewish school.
We have 52 boys, and their uniforms were stolen off of a truck.
And we need to make their uniforms all over again.
I'm looking for a piece of blue fabric-- a certain shade of blue and a certain weight of blue.
I says-- she wants to buy it wholesale.
She doesn't have a resale number and all that.
I say, that's no problem.
Let's go in the back.
And I'm showing her this fabric-- this was too dark.
This was too light.
This was too heavy.
I didn't have the right fabric.
And she looks besides herself.
And she says, well, she's ready to leave.
I said, wait a minute.
I say, I've got one of my-- it's a competitor.
But I'm sure they carry all kinds of fabric in, in solid color.
Where is your car parked?
So she tells me, about five blocks away.
I say, come with me.
I take her with my car.
And I take her down to 26th Street and Grand Avenue.
And I go into Central Textile.
And I says, Alex-- the owner-- I said, Alex, this lady-- and I tell her story.
I said, I want you to donate the fabric to them.
Oh, Andy, no problem.
Take her in the back, and tell Joe that this is-- we're looking and looking.
Finally, she finds the exact fabric.
Joseph, can you come back in two hours?
I'll have it ready for you because I can't stop the machines now.
So-- and she looks-- what is she going to do for two hours?
I said, listen, give me your address.
And on my way home, I'll bring it home to you.
I brought her back to her car.
She couldn't get over it.
How is a nice guy like you is not married yet?
[LAUGHS] I was-- I was 36 years old.
I was a confirmed bachelor.
So about a month later, I get a phone call.
This is Rose.
And I forgot all about it.
You remember you-- oh, yes, yeah.
Listen, I got a very nice girl for you.
Write this phone number down, and so on.
And that's how I met my wife.
[LAUGHTER]
I finally called her.
And you have two--
I have a daughter and a son-- a son and a daughter, right.
My son is 30-- he was 30 in January.
And his name is Gary.
And my daughter was 28 in January.
Her name is Karen.
The reason they were both born in January-- in April, I'm not a busy man.
I didn't travel anywhere.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, more or less.
I've been working in the textile business for my uncle in New York.
And then, when I moved out here, I worked-- for the first one year, I worked for Sears, Roebuck Company.
I didn't know how to travel.
I didn't know my way around.
I figured that's the best way to find out.
And I went to work there.
I was selling refrigerators, washing machines.
The first three months, I won the first prize as the biggest-- the best salesman.
It was a $25 bond.
This is in 1952.
And after that, I, I went up to the manager.
I says, I'm quitting.
I need to get back into my-- and the store manager came down.
What's this I hear, that you're quitting?
You're one of our best salesmen, and so on, he says.
So I told them, this is not what I intended to do.
My heart is in textile, and so on.
And I walked into this company cold.
And I'm looking for a job.
And he says, well, it just so happens we have an opening.
How much money would you feel like that we have to pay you for-- and I says, I'm not asking for any money.
Just pay me commission.
If I sell, you pay me commission.
Well, in the meantime, you need-- you need money for gasoline, they said.
And I said, I have enough money.
I just-- I'm not-- I didn't want to accept anything.
This was unusual to him.
After, like, three months or so, he calls me in the office.
His name was [?
Dalos ?] [?
McDonald. ?] He says, Andrew, you've been doing good.
And, he says, I want you to sell your car and go down to whatever Chevrolet company.
And they're going to give you a company car.
And that will be your car until you work here.
It's a station wagon.
So I go down there.
And I come back.
Well, how do you like the car?
I said, I didn't get it.
I didn't accept it.
Why not?
Because it has the company name on the side of the door.
I says, I'm dating a girl.
I don't want to go to a drive-in movie with a company car with the name on there.
He starts smiling.
He says, you know, you're right.
He says, go back there.
It won't be on there.
[LAUGHS] So that's how I got a car.
Another three months later, he says to me-- he says, from now on, if you get into-- any town you get into-- like, I used to go down to Calexico, Mexicali, you know, in the Imperial Valley.
He said, I want you to stop the first policeman.
And I'm looking at him.
He says, ask him which is the best hotel.
That's where I want you to stay.
And you just hand in your, your expense sheet to the girl.
And she'll reimburse you.
And this is going on.
Three years later, he says-- he calls me.
And he says, among all the salesmen, you are one person that I have to find a job for.
I'm, I'm-- I decided to retire.
The man was in his late '40s.
He was a rich man.
He didn't need that, that business.
And that's how he connected me with the company that I'm with now.
That was in 1955.
And I've been with them ever since.
And ever since 1980, I'm the national sales manager.
Well, I had to for my family.
Yeah.
No, not about not wanting to live, and so on.
I'm glad that I was left alive.
And I'm, I'm glad for what I have.
I cherish it.
I'm sure they are.
No, she was born in Boston.
Well, they are well aware of what happened to me.
They were watching the very first time the Holocaust pictures came out.
And if they are affected one way or another, I personally can-- cannot tell it as much because I'm too close to them.
Oh, yes.
They--
They asked me questions, also.
In the beginning, they asked me, what's this number on your arm?
I used to tell them it's my European phone number.
[LAUGHTER] That's when they were too young for me to tell them, you know.
So--
Well, especially my, my son.
He, he also is very emotional.
He has a lot of feeling.
He knows exactly what I went through.
And I can see, sometimes, he, he goes through that same pain.
When he went to see it was painful for him.
But he wanted to see it.
And he was glad to see it.
He knows what I went through.
[SIGHS] I don't think so.
My wife was too close to me to wake me up.
You know, my, my screaming nightmares, those happened two, three weeks after I got to New York, you know.
So after that, when I started going to a psychiatrist, those things have diminished.
There she is.
Well, what is important for-- generally, for people to realize is that hatred can do a lot of cruel things.
And this is something they should always be aware of, that it just doesn't lead to anything good.
Keep it out of yourself.
It's always easier to love than to hate.
And I don't want to get into the issues of race and religion, and so on.
But really, everyone ought to be the same.
I personally was surprised at the beginning-- that was odd and looked-- in 1948, I made a trip to Florida with some of my friends.
I was working as a waiter in the Catskill Mountains.
I'd saved up some money.
So we went to Florida afterwards.
And I saw that the colored people had to sit in the back of the bus.
And I rebelled against that.
Why?
You know, they're, they're, they're-- just because they're black, they have to sit in the back?
I kept silent to myself.
But I, I never thought it was-- they call this a democratic country.
This is not democratic, you know.
I was-- I read, at that time, the book of And that kind of shook me up a lot.
Well, the Jewish community was very active in, in the civil rights movement, so--
Yeah.
Talking about the Jewish community, I traveled all over Europe and the major-- what are you looking at?
Judy?
We traveled with my cousin Andy to every major city in, in Europe, I guess either to look for additional survivors.
We were allowed to travel free of charge everywhere.
Well, yeah.
Because the only one I definitely heard about is, is my younger brother, who was with my cousin Andy--
--in Warsaw.
And the way he stayed alive-- remained alive-- one morning-- or one day, they picked him among 20 or 25 other people on a detail.
You, you, you, you-- come with me.
And he was taken outside the camp.
And it was a Warsaw, Warsaw camp.
My brother was inside.
And, and just as he was outside, working on some detail-- I don't know what they were doing, digging ditches or whatever-- the Polish people staged an uprising against the Germans.
And shooting and chaos left and right-- and he quickly took, took off civilian clothes from a dead person.
He spoke good Polish.
And he just mingled in among the-- he lived the rest of the war out on a farm.
The farmer probably suspected that he's Jewish.
But he was doing his work just for the food.
And, and, and he slept in the barn, or whatever.
And that's how he remained alive.
But he told me also that my brother was inside the camp.
Everybody that was inside was either killed or-- they, they set the camp on fire.
And so I know that my brother didn't make it that way.
But about my older brother, or the youngest brother, while I suspected they-- they had no use for seven-year-old kids, other than, God forbid, a, a, a lampshade.
Who knows?
Maybe by some freak thing, maybe he's alive.
Somebody saved him.
We traveled all over.
We wound-- we wound up five days in Bucharest, Romania.
We travelled.
We went into Budapest.
In fact, we were there for quite a while.
These are languages that-- we spoke the language of, of these countries.
So--
